text: The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing multiple adhesions from the previous surgery. These adhesions are carefully lysed using a monopolar hook.
The surgeon opens a posterior window and places a penrose drain for traction and exposure. Dissection progresses into the mediastinum, achieving complete mobilization of the esophagus.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the diaphragmatic pillars on both the interior and posterior aspects. The hiatus is repaired using four interrupted stitches with zero non-absorbable suture.
The surgeon dissects the complete circumference of the gastric tube and uses two stapler loads to transect it, visibly separating the gastric tissue.
The surgeon retracts the transverse colon to identify the angle of Treitz. The jejunum is measured fifty centimeters from this angle and transected using a surgical stapler.

The surgeon measures 120 centimeters on the alimentary limb and performs enterotomies using a monopolar hook. A side-to-side anastomosis is created with a stapler, followed by oversewing the enterotomies in two layers with 3-0 absorbable suture.

The surgeon begins the gastrojejunostomy by placing a continuous posterior outer layer of 3-0 absorbable suture. Enterotomies are created using a monopolar hook. A continuous inner-posterior layer is then sutured with 3-0 absorbable suture. After completing the posterior layer, the surgeon starts an inner-anterior layer, and a second suture is initiated from the opposite side, meeting the first suture in the middle for reconstruction.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis by performing the outer anterior layer using absorbable sutures, finalizing the gastrojejunostomy reconstruction.
